reason: Strategy to employ slightly fewer people while increasing compensation and productivity, citing rising staff costs and efforts to improve revenue per employee.

Angi announced ~350 layoffs to 'reduce operating expenses and optimize the organizational structure in support of long-term growth and in light of AI-driven efficiency improvements.' Expected to save $70-80M annually with ~$30M in restructuring charges. Most engineers among affected; ML algorithms now handle 'a substantial portion of what was once manual labor.'
Redesigning its go-to-market approach to serve customers more effectively through intelligent, customer-led execution, reorganizing account management teams by customer segment.
Strategic repositioning to expand into robotics and Physical AI while reducing investment in other areas adopting the technology more slowly; not financial distress.
Efficiency measures driven in part by the growing adoption of AI tools, reflecting broader efficiency efforts across the tech sector.
source not accessible

Semiconductor test equipment maker FormFactor announced 200+ layoffs and facility closures (Baldwin Park CA closing Jan 30 with 113 jobs; Carlsbad closing mid-Dec with 107 jobs). Company cited import tariffs and changes in market demand. Expects $30-40M in restructuring charges; affects technicians, engineers, managers, and assemblers.

MercadoLibre laid off 119 people (originally 116 reported) due to AI expansion, primarily UX writers - the team behind app/website copy. Affected 38 in Brazil and 32 in Argentina. Cofounder Marcos Galperin said his focus would shift to AI projects and mastering 'this transition to the world of agents.' Some affected workers reported training the AI that replaced them.

CTO Andrew Bosworth memo: Meta is 'shifting some of our investment from Metaverse toward Wearables' to make the business 'more sustainable,' reinvesting savings into wearables/AR (smart glasses, controllers). ~10% of Reality Labs cut; AR roles were preserved while VR/metaverse investment was scaled back. Reflects the broader pivot from metaverse to AI.

Part of eToro's maturation process to align organizational structure with the business and support long-term growth, framed as restructuring from a position of strength to focus on future technologies.

No specific reason stated; remaining team is focused on maintaining existing projects and commercializing fast-charging battery technology, suggesting workforce streamlining ahead of SPAC merger and Nasdaq listing.
CEO said the broad growth mindset is no longer sustainable; Playtika is restructuring to focus on fewer games, reduce headcount, and implement AI and automation.
Strategic repositioning to bundle core competencies and concentrate resources on areas with the highest growth potential following an organizational strategy review.

Press manager Ralf Begner cited competitive pressure in telecom: 'the market has not shown any growth while inflationary pressure continues, and the company must continue to work on reducing its costs to ensure its long-term technology leadership.' Driven by a sharp decline in 5G network investments globally as carriers shift from rollout to monetization. Cuts hit core business and R&D in Sweden (>1 in 10 of 12,600 Swedish staff).

Israeli fintech Tipalti began another layoff round (industry estimates: 100+ employees) primarily affecting customer success, sales development, and engineering, with ~40% in R&D in Tel Aviv. Company cited economic headwinds and need to optimize operational efficiency. This was the second wave in ~2 months following a sales-restructuring round.
Layoffs were due to role overlaps from the Coinme and Sequence acquisitions being integrated into Polygon Labs, while keeping overall headcount flat.
Layoffs follow a broader global restructuring effort after Vimeo's acquisition by Bending Spoons; no specific underlying cause was stated in the article.

Vimeo laid off more than 1,000 employees globally - the entire video engineering team was eliminated, with fewer than 15 engineers remaining. Came two months after Italian PE/conglomerate Bending Spoons closed its $1.38B acquisition. Matches Bending Spoons' pattern: WeTransfer saw 75% of staff terminated within two months of its 2024 acquisition, and Filmic's entire 22-person team was fired in 2023.

CEO Andrew Anagnost framed the ~7% cut (mostly customer-facing sales) as completion of a multi-year go-to-market transformation, with smaller targeted adjustments to align with future opportunities. Resources reallocated to AI, platform, and cloud initiatives. Restructuring charges estimated at $135-160M, mostly severance.
Shopify is restructuring its partnerships division to focus on building low-friction systems and concentrating efforts on agentic commerce to help merchants capture the AI opportunity.
The company terminated employees citing 'Code 18 - End of Work', allegedly used to circumvent standard mass layoff procedures; no substantive business rationale was publicly stated.
Entropy is winding down because the company wasn't venture scale; after multiple pivots and layoffs, the founder couldn't find a viable business model supporting venture-level growth.

Expedia filed WARN notice Jan 28 cutting 162 tech roles at Seattle Interbay HQ (~10% of tech team) - software engineers, data engineers, product/program managers. Statement: 'We are eliminating roles as well as opening some new roles as we remain disciplined about assessing the skills we need for the future. We are also simplifying our structure and reducing organizational layers to move faster and with more accountability.' Cited competition and need to enhance AI-driven experiences.

CEO Bill Ready told staff the cuts were to 'double down on an AI-forward approach,' redirecting resources to AI-focused roles and prioritizing AI-powered products. Also scaling back office space, with $35-45M in pre-tax restructuring charges expected by end of Q3. Stock plummeted on the news. Notable aftermath: Pinterest later fired engineers who built an internal layoff-tracking tool.
Strategic restructuring with targeted streamlining in two units following internal review of operational structure and processes, framed as cost optimization amid continued growth.
Amazon is strengthening its organization by reducing layers, increasing ownership, and removing bureaucracy across corporate roles.
ASML is streamlining its Technology and IT organizations to address inefficiencies and slow process flows, so engineers can focus on engineering and innovation.

Kiwi.com cut 250 of 700 employees (36%) in its second round since 2024. CEO Oliver Dlouhy told staff: 'Delivering both, at scale and over time, requires clear priorities, fast execution, and a structure designed to support the next phase of Kiwi.com,' and that the company will operate 'with a smaller team, optimized for speed and autonomy.' Refocusing on low fares.
source not accessible

Zupee cut ~200 employees in a second phase of cost rationalization following India's August 2025 Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, which banned real-money gaming. Total cuts now ~370 across two rounds. Primarily affected tech and customer support; Zupee Studio (micro-drama vertical) was spared. Continuing health insurance and offering career transition support.

Peloton cut 11% of staff in a cost-cutting move primarily affecting engineering and enterprise teams. CEO Peter Stern informed staffers; second major reduction in six months after a 6% August 2025 cut. Targeting at least $100M in annual spending cuts through 2026. Came months after the weak-selling October 2025 Cross Training Series launch with Peloton IQ AI hardware.
Expedia is simplifying its structure and reducing organizational layers to move faster, while remaining disciplined about assessing the skills it needs for the future.

Zillow let go ~200 employees (about 2%) in performance-based reductions tied to its annual review process. Spokesperson: 'After thoughtful consideration, we made the decision to separate a small number of employees whose performance did not meet expectations,' adding 'This decision is not connected to market conditions or recent business developments.' Primarily engineering, product, and sales.
Parent Avis Budget Group is consolidating Zipcar's Boston headquarters to New Jersey as part of broader corporate restructuring to make Zipcar run more efficiently in the long term.

Workday cut 400 employees (about 2%) concentrated almost entirely in Global Customer Operations (Customer Success, Adoption Services, Customer Support, implementation enablement). Disclosed via regulatory filing; restructuring expected to drive ~$135M in charges and be completed by late April 2026. Follows a much larger 1,600-job round one year earlier.
Parent Remitly is consolidating R&D to other global locations to streamline operations, reduce complexity, and focus on its most significant growth engines in cross-border money transfers.

Smartsheet quietly cut staff in Feb 2026 affecting engineers, marketing managers, project managers and others. Statement: company 'recently made organizational changes to better align our resources with our long-term business priorities.' Headcount of cut not disclosed. Follows ~120-person October round and Smartsheet's $8.4B take-private deal by Blackstone/Vista, with investors known for pushing higher margins.

Salesforce quietly laid off fewer than 1,000 roles on Feb 10, 2026, mostly in marketing, product management, data analytics, Agentforce, and Heroku - not formally announced. Came amid executive reshuffle: 6 new execs replacing 5 who exited since December, with Joe Inzerillo leading the merger of Agentforce and Slack. Benioff has previously cited cutting customer support from ~9,000 to ~5,000 via AI tools.
source not accessible

Under new CEO Colin Walsh, Glossier said it is being 'reshaped' into smaller, more agile teams to restore operational discipline, sharpen brand positioning, and reignite growth after years of slowed momentum.
source not accessible
Restructuring after the SalesLoft merger to eliminate overlapping sales, marketing, and engineering roles and accelerate investment in AI-powered revenue intelligence capabilities.
Company said it is undergoing organizational restructuring to improve efficiency and sharpen business focus, with some staff being integrated into other business units.
Company said the cuts were part of refining its organizational structure and ensuring operational discipline and efficiency as it matures and prepares for an IPO.
Management said expanded AI-driven capabilities now reduce the need for a large engineering organization, citing a strategic shift to improve efficiency in a new technological era.
No explicit public statement; layoffs appear tied to post-merger consolidation with Palo Alto Networks, focused on overlapping sales, operational, and administrative roles created by the combination.
Skillsoft did not publicly detail the rationale; the entire Codecademy curriculum team was eliminated, confirmed via employee LinkedIn posts rather than an official statement.
Company attributed the cuts to integration of advanced AI agents and automation across sales, operations, design, and marketing, framing it as a strategic AI pivot rather than cost-cutting.
Interim CEO Marc Winterhoff said the cuts will 'improve operational effectiveness and optimize our resources' as Lucid pursues profitability.
CEO Zubin Appoo declared 'the era of manually writing code as the core act of engineering is over,' with AI displacing software development and customer service work across 40 countries.

After going private under founder Scott Painter, TrueCar refocused on profitability and its core mission, eliminating unprofitable initiatives (TrueCar+, TCMS, TCWS) that had consumed over half of company resources.

Company is reducing general and administrative costs amid a broader reorganization; AI is central to its evolving cost structure (automated RFPs, coding assistance, chatbots) and prediction market competition pressures.

CEO Stephen Ehikian said the cost structure was 'simply too high' after aggressive 2024-25 hiring outpaced slower enterprise AI adoption; also cites AI-driven efficiency. Came alongside a wider loss and stock drop.

DoorDash, which acquired Deliveroo, closed Qatar and Singapore operations following a review of country-specific conditions, framed as aligning with long-term value for customers, merchants, and riders.

CEO Jack Dorsey said Block is shifting to operate with 'smaller, highly talented teams using AI to automate more work,' explicitly stating the business is strong and the cuts are an AI-driven structural change.
Targeting GBP150M in technology and support cost savings to achieve cash-flow positivity in fiscal 2025/26; cuts focused on UK head-office tech and back-office roles, including roughly half from R&D.
Company said it is reinvesting and aligning its structure with strategic priorities, based on operating model needs and areas of duplication; cuts followed eBay's $1.2B Depop acquisition.
CTO described it as an 'AI-driven efficiency shift,' aligning operating costs with revenue-generating activities and trimming the team from 18 to 10 to match product priorities.

Post-Thoma Bravo acquisition consolidation: Verint is being combined with Calabrio to build an AI-driven CX platform, with cuts focused on overlapping roles, overhead, and tightening R&D/go-to-market budgets.

Cuts were part of a plan to consolidate Redmond engineering, manufacturing, supply chain, and quality activities into a new Orlando facility to reduce operating expenses and align resources with strategic priorities.

Hyundai-backed eVTOL startup called the cuts 'a strategic pivot to ensure our staffing and cost structures are optimized for the long-term delivery of our market-aligned aircraft design' after lagging competitors and pausing flight testing.
CEO Hichame Assi cited the need to respond to rapidly changing customer expectations and to reduce operating costs in order to invest further in product, marketing, and AI.

Robotics unit cuts attributed to routine organizational reviews to ensure teams are set up to innovate and deliver for customers; broader Amazon reductions have been tied to AI-driven operational effectiveness.
CEO Rotem Iram said the R&D layoffs were part of a transition to operating profit while maintaining At-Bay's position in cyber insurance.
Company reported 'economic reasons' to the Quebec government as the basis for terminations, with some staff previously on temporary layoff status.
Company said the cuts were part of its regular annual performance review process, through which a small percentage of employees transition out.
No publicly stated reason; cuts were concentrated in the digital wealth division (financial planning, advisor and client experience software).
CEO Mike Cannon-Brookes said the company is pivoting toward AI and reorganizing for the AI era, changing the mix of skills and roles needed.
Company said the layoffs were part of restructuring aimed at greater efficiency, including advances in AI initiatives that contributed to the ~3% headcount reduction.
Cited market conditions and a need to better align the organization with areas of greatest impact on product, customer experience, and sustainable growth.
CEO Justin Mezzell said sophisticated AI agents and bot spam had overwhelmed Digg's user-voting model, making it impossible to trust content rankings, forcing a retool and app shutdown.

Annual filing attributed the 10% workforce reduction to ongoing restructuring and cost controls (reorganizations, limited external hiring) as Dell shifts investment toward AI-optimized servers while managing weak PC demand.
Chicago franchise-investing startup shut down operations (100% staff impacted); specific public rationale was not detailed beyond the company-wide closure.
CEO Kris Marszalek said the cuts targeted 'roles that do not adapt' to AI, framing it as an urgent pivot to embed AI across operations or risk being left behind.

Eliminated its entire technical writing and documentation team (~70 roles) as 'targeted adjustments' tied to its AI strategy, freeing budget for AI talent and Cortex agents following a $200M OpenAI partnership that automated documentation work.

Company cited deploying AI tools to increase productivity amid heavy losses (~$587M-$602M for 2025), market exits (UK, EEA, Australia), and a pivot to prediction markets and a 'super app' model to support a path to profitability.

Spotify said the podcast group cuts were intended to improve execution, speed, and alignment across teams, and were 'not about cost-cutting,' with continued investment in multiformat content and video.

Cited a downturn in Fortnite engagement starting in 2025 that caused spending to outpace revenue, along with industry-wide headwinds (slower growth, weaker spending, console softness); Epic explicitly said the cuts were not AI-related.

Zendesk conducted cuts in late March 2026 amid its AI push - completed acquisition of Forethought (an AI agent platform) on March 26, 2026, enabling autonomous AI agents that handle ~80% of support tickets. Specific public reporting on the March 24 internal memo not surfaced; cuts align with broader Zendesk strategy of shifting customer support functions to AI agents (a continuation of multiple PE-era rounds).
Company spokesperson said it regularly evaluates its organization to ensure alignment with strategic priorities and long-term growth.

~700 jobs cut across Facebook social, global operations, recruiting, sales, and Reality Labs (the biggest hit). Internal memo framed it as sharpening operations to free up resources for AI; some employees offered new roles (some requiring relocation). Coincided with record AI capex guidance ($125-145B for 2026, more than 2x 2025), shifting resources away from AR hardware.
Restructuring to align with AI-driven productivity gains, reorganizing to be flatter and faster with AI-first products and workflows.
Decision to cease operations in a planned and orderly manner; underlying business factors not disclosed in the article.
Dissolved an entire customer service department (Customer GmbH) and restructured it, with affected employees asked to reapply for their jobs; underlying business rationale not detailed.

Mass layoff (~18% of 162k workforce) executed without HR/manager warning to redirect headcount spend into a ~$50B FY26 AI capex push (Nvidia GB300 racks, AMD MI355X clusters, and the 4.5GW Stargate data-center buildout tied to OpenAI's $300B compute contract). Larry Ellison is betting Oracle's next decade on more compute; the cuts happened despite Q3 FY26 GAAP net income of $3.7B (+27% YoY) and RPO of $553B (+325% YoY).
source not accessible
Persistent cash crunch and inability to secure funding, compounded by the CTO's resignation and two attempted acquisition deals falling through.
Shutdown due to inability to reach profitability amid the shift in the VR market and broader gaming headwinds, with expenses outpacing revenue despite 150M players.
Shutdown due to failure to reach strong product-market fit as AI models improved rapidly and the industry shifted toward specialized experts rather than crowdsourced feedback.

Sonos cut numerous marketing staff as new CMO Colleen DeCourcy consolidated marketing into five core global functions. DeCourcy described the prior structure as 'overly fragmented, with overlapping roles and multiple handoffs.' Framed as simplification rather than cost-cutting; not performance related. Coincided with launch of first new consumer hardware in over a year. Specific headcount not disclosed.
source not accessible
source not accessible
Reorganization and a change in focus, coinciding with recent CEO and C-level leadership changes.
Shutdown due to limited business penetration, rising competition from large tech players' advanced image generation models, and financial strain from a lost major client.

Vimeo filed a WARN notice April 1 to lay off 132 employees at its 330 W. 34th St Midtown Manhattan office, effective April 20 - about 25% of NYC staff. Second major reduction since Bending Spoons closed its $1.38B acquisition in late 2025; follows the January round that eliminated most of the global staff including core video engineering.
Becoming a leaner organization that leverages AI more centrally, citing that operating in 2026 is very different from prior years.

GoPro board approved a restructuring plan eliminating 145 jobs (23% of 631-person workforce) to lower costs and improve operating margins. Plan costs $11.5-15M in severance/healthcare. Follows 2025 revenue decline including $9M Q4 loss. Company is also reportedly weighing a sale or merger.
Reorganization amid a dramatically changing technology landscape, 'refounding' the company to move quickly and serve clients building AI agents.
Consolidating corporate functions with its People Inc. business as part of a rebrand and reorganization, expected to save about $40M annually.

Bankruptcy filing; company had only $665K in assets against $3.4M in liabilities, including approximately $2.9M owed to TSYS/Global Payments.
source not accessible
Organizational restructuring to align with technological developments and newly signed customer agreements, following the sale of Esh Bank to Isracard.
source not accessible
Shifting focus from infrastructure and research toward revenue-generating products, becoming more agile and commercially focused.
Restructuring to achieve profitability, with the company targeting cash-generative status by June 2026.

CEO Evan Spiegel: company has reached 'a crucible moment' requiring 'a new way of working that is faster and more efficient.' Cited AI as helping staff work faster, reducing 'repetitive work' and enabling smaller teams. 16% of global workforce plus ~300 open roles closed; targets $500M+ in annualized cost savings by H2 2026. Stock jumped on the news.

CEO Jennifer Morgan (joined 2024) is restructuring UKG into an 'AI-first company,' citing the need to streamline operations and realign resources with evolving customer demands. ~600 employees left immediately, ~350 stayed for transition. Hits Sunrise and Weston, FL offices hard; follows a prior 2,200-person (14%) cut in late 2024. Owned by PE firm Hellman & Friedman since 2019.
Strategic realignment; the company is continuing to hire in parallel while accelerating expansion of its DeeperDive product with major media partners.
Restructuring to remove duplication, simplify operations, and capture efficiencies following two major acquisitions that increased company size and complexity.
Ongoing management processes and organizational adjustments in line with market developments and customer needs, framed as routine efficiency work.
Transformation into an AI-only organization focused on its Productboard Spark product, citing that AI is collapsing the process layer of building software.
Meta terminated a major contract with Sama; the company unsuccessfully tried to retain the workstream, forcing redundancies for its Nairobi staff.
Restructuring to streamline work and accelerate delivery, consolidating web and app development teams in the US instead of Israel.

Quora laid off employees across teams - particularly Poe-focused teams and shared SG&A. CEO Adam D'Angelo: 'Poe had been funding its growth with support from Quora, and we decided that it was time for Poe to stand on its own financially and fund its own growth.' Also: 'The world is changing rapidly with the rise of AI, and we see incredible potential to apply it to greatly improve both of our products, while maintaining financial independence for both of them through smaller, more focused teams.'
Structural realignment to reset internal operations around AI, framed as a pre-IPO restructuring rather than cost-cutting.

EQT-backed Epidemic Sound cut 150 jobs (one-third of staff) to adapt to AI. Interim CEO Sara Borsvik said the move is 'not financially driven' but to align with AI development; company will broaden offering with AI support and eventually offer entirely AI-generated music. Board chair stated 'in principle all previous business models will become obsolete.'
Strategic evolution to streamline operations, incorporate AI into daily workflows, and stay ahead of shifts in the regulatory and technology landscape.
Declining demand and challenging macroeconomic conditions, including weaker sales and rising component costs driven by the AI industry.
Restructuring to improve operational efficiency and align the company with its long-term AI-focused roadmap, accelerating its transition into an AI-first organization.
Focus on core activities and continued development of AI-driven cybersecurity verification products, reallocating resources.
source not accessible

New CEO Enrique Lores (ex-HP Inc., took the helm March 1, 2026) announced cutting ~20% of the 23,800-person workforce as part of a 2-3 year reorg targeting at least $1.5B in annualized run-rate savings and a shift to an 'AI-native operating model.' Announced alongside weaker-than-expected Q1 profit.
theme_original: cost cutting / AI-native reorg under new CEO

reason: Adjusting cost structure amid crypto market downturn while leveraging AI tools so smaller teams can do more.

Freshworks cut 11% of its workforce (about 500 jobs) to 'streamline organizational efforts and product development, and increase leverage of AI and automation.' CEO Dennis Woodside said 'over half of our code is written by AI' and that automation reduced 'rote work that technology can take care of.' Cuts span global teams and aim to reduce management layers; announced same day as Q1 2026 earnings beat.

Citing rising inflation, weakening demand, intense competition, and global capital constraints; exiting Pakistan ride-hailing and broader global cost-cutting restructure.
Better aligning structure and investments with long-term strategy; redirecting resources to its Superintelligence platform and Agentic SOC.
Framed as regular performance-based reviews leading to role-specific changes; reportedly aimed at increasing revenue per employee.

AI-led automation rendering some roles redundant, combined with relocation of product and technology teams from Bengaluru to Gurugram.
source not accessible
Reorganizing to operate effectively in the agentic AI era; internal AI usage grew over 600% in three months, requiring fundamental restructuring of roles and processes.
Aligning cost structure and talent with growth trajectory, investing more deliberately in highest-impact areas to improve organizational agility.

Ticketmaster cut ~350 jobs (8%) across 25 countries, heaviest in engineering, then product and design. Global President Saumil Mehta framed AI as 'a new utility' for mobile, search and pricing, and said the reorg aimed to 'flatten organizational layers' and speed decisions. Came one day after a 10% Q1 revenue jump to $765M.
Restructuring as an enduring global AI company with smaller, AI-embedded teams to match accelerating pace of AI advancement.

Upwork cut ~24% of workforce. CEO Hayden Brown's memo: 'Two pizza teams are dead. AI means smaller, differently resourced teams in product and engineering can make a bigger impact than ever.' Also: 'Fewer layers means faster decisions. Flatter is better.' Cited slower near-term growth in Q1 and profitability goals.
source not accessible

Chemnitz-based unicorn Staffbase cut 176 people (22%) - 'every fifth position' - driven by economic pressure (inflation, investor caution), shifts in industry demand, and internal restructuring. Company is consolidating product development to Berlin and Saxony, closing other sites. Frames it as reducing complexity after three acquisitions.
Filed for bankruptcy after failed acquisition negotiations; CEO hinted at over-hiring and reactive decisions as contributing factors.
Restructuring to redirect spending toward AI-enabled engineering and data infrastructure, targeting ~$60M in annual savings; cuts concentrated in R&D and downmarket sales.
Structural adjustments to optimize financial performance and operational execution ahead of planned IPO.

PE-backed real estate software firm MRI cut ~200 roles globally, explicitly citing AI adoption across products and operations. Company framed it as 'strategic alignment of resources' to improve scalability. Cuts come as PE owners (TA Associates, Harvest Partners, GI Partners) explore a sale/IPO at up to $10B valuation with Goldman Sachs advising.
Closing German development office, relocating work abroad; CTO claimed German team was not open enough to new development approaches and the company needed to act quickly.
Reducing European headcount by 20% to relocate closer to US-headquartered customers, who now account for 75% of revenue.
Non-renewal of contracts for 58 engineering, product, and data staff at scheduled expiry; no substantive strategic justification provided publicly.
Restructuring to shift investment toward areas of strongest demand in the AI era, including silicon, optics, security, and AI.

New CEO Daniel Shapero (ex-COO, took over month prior) memo: 'We need to reinvent how we work, with agile teams focused on our highest priorities, and by shifting investments toward areas such as infrastructure to fulfill our mission and vision over the long term.' Spokesperson called it 'regular business planning.' Source told Reuters AI automation was NOT the cause. Notable because LinkedIn just crossed $5B quarterly revenue (+12% YoY).
theme_original: restructuring under new CEO (explicitly not AI-driven)

reason: Reducing headcount and process automation through expansion of AI tools to improve operational efficiency and cut costs.
source not accessible
Transitioning to an AI-native company and aligning the team to current business priorities to become lean, fast and focused.

Dune Analytics cut 25% of staff (~35 of 143) as cofounder Fredrik Haga restructures around AI agents and institutional onchain finance adoption. Pivot leans on Dune MCP (open-standard server launched March 2026) that lets AI agents query its data warehouse via natural language. Company says it remains well capitalized after $79M total raised.
AI Transformation-led restructure making AI the foundation of team structures and processes, targeting $13M in annual savings.

Kraken parent Payward cut ~150 workers after deploying AI that improved efficiency, primarily in customer service, compliance, and trading support. Kraken's AI chatbot now handles ~80% of customer inquiries. IPO timeline may slip from late 2026 to 2027 due to crypto market decline; Kraken paused its confidential SEC filing in March.

Israeli AI startup AI21 Labs (Amnon Shashua) cut 110 of 180 employees (~61%) and pivoted away from selling foundation models toward its Maestro AI agent orchestration platform. Remaining staff are mostly research and product. Cited inability of foundation models to be a sufficient source of income; revenue agreements include deals with Nebius and Wix.

Zuckerberg memo: 'Success isn't a given' and 'AI is the most consequential technology of our lifetimes.' Roughly 10% of staff cut while ~7,000 employees were redeployed into new AI-focused roles (Applied AI Engineering, Agent Transformation Accelerator, Central Analytics). AI infrastructure, foundation-model, and AI monetization teams were explicitly protected. Zuckerberg said no further companywide layoffs were expected in 2026.

CEO Sasan Goodarzi internal memo: cuts (17% of ~18,200 employees) framed as 'reducing complexity and simplifying the corporate structure' to deliver better products and consolidate teams in key hubs (winding down Reno and Woodland Hills offices). Notably Goodarzi told CNBC 'None of it had to do with AI... Everything was about how do we become more effective,' even though the restructuring funds AI integration across TurboTax/QuickBooks/Credit Karma.

ClickUp cut ~290 of 1,300 employees (22%) and introduced $1M salary bands for survivors. CEO Zeb Evans framed it on X not as cost-cutting but as a structural bet on AI: restructuring around a '100x org' model where AI agents outnumber employees 3:1. The company now runs ~3,000 internal AI agents. Path to $1M salary available to anyone producing '100x impact' by creating/managing AI systems.
Weak earnings, nearly 50% stock decline, rising AI compute costs from Base44 acquisition, and roles becoming redundant in the AI era.

14% workforce reduction and exit from 22 countries to fund AI infrastructure buildout and platform re-architecture for agentic workloads. CEO Bill Staples: partnered with an AI lab to rebuild infra for AI-scale traffic. Cuts announced alongside +23% YoY Q1 revenue ($264.2M) with positive non-GAAP margin.

CEO Vlad Tenev memo cut ~10% of workforce (290 people) framed as forward-looking use of 'frontier technologies to push our execution even further'. $28M restructuring charge. Headcount had grown 2,300 (Dec 2024) to ~2,900 (2025), a 26% jump before the cut — classic AI-era hire-then-correct pattern.
Sony-owned Bungie (Destiny, Halo legacy) cut ~50% of remaining staff as Destiny 2 winds down and studio reorganizes around Marathon and future titles. Franchise decline, not AI-related.

Xbox division restructuring: 3,200 cuts (1,600 immediate + 1,600 to follow) plus divestment of 4-5 studios (Ninja Theory, Undead Labs, Compulsion Games, Double Fine). Xbox CEO Asha Sharma: 'our business today is not healthy', operating margins 3-10x lower than comparable businesses. Called the 'most significant restructure in Xbox history'.

Sales, consulting, and back-office cuts (~1,600 of the 4,800 total July 6 announcement, remainder is Xbox). Timed to fiscal year start (FY26). Corporate rationale: free capital for AI capex; FY26 forecast >$100B vs $80B in FY25.
